coredata.dc:title: Application of polyamide 56 nanofiber membrane loaded with coffee grounds carbon dots in Fe3+ detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Dai, Lixing
Chen, Guoqiang
coredata.dc:description: One novel nanofiber membrane was fabricated which could detect Fe3+ with the help of either fluorescence spectrometer or smartphone. Coffee grounds carbon dots (CCDs) were prepared by the solvent-thermal method, followed by the fabrication of CCDs/polyamide 56 (PA56) composite nanofiber membrane through electrospinning process. The 4 % CCDs/PA56 composite nanofiber membrane (FNM4) maintained good fluorescence performance (λem = 554 nm, λex = 470 nm) even after 5-runs quenching-recovery cycles. Its fluorescence could be quenched by Fe3+ with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.74 μM when the fluorescence spectrometer was used. In addition, FNM4 could also detect Fe3+ by reading its RGB values using one smartphone and the lowest LOD was 4.31 μM when one Apple smartphone was used. The second method is portable to operate and eliminates the constraints of bulky laboratory equipment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon dots
Coffee grounds
CCDs/PA56 composite nanofiber membrane
Fe3+ detection
Smartphone platform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Monitor Fe3+ in different water sources
Quenching mechanism study of CCD dispersion
CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane for Fe3+ detection
Fe3+ detection based on fluorescence spectrometer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman spectroscopy and (c) FT-IR spectrum of CCDs powder. High-resolution XPS of (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) N 1s for CCDs powder.

Photoluminescence properties of the CCDs dispersion in ethanol. (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence excitation & emission spectrum. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CCDs dispersion under different excitation lights. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra and (d) CIE coordinates of CCDs dispersion at different concentrations (λex = 400 nm).

(a) Digital images, (b) fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 400 nm) and (c) fluorescence intensity ratios I0/I of CCDs dispersion after adding different metal salt solutions (1000 μM). (d) Digital images, (e) fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 400 nm) and (f) fluorescence intensity ratios I0/I of CCDs dispersion after adding FeCl3 solution with different concentrations (0–3000 μM).

The UV absorption spectra of (a) FeCl3 solution (Vethanol: Vwater = 1:1) and (b) CCDs mixture after adding FeCl3 aqueous solution (0–3000 μM). Fluorescence attenuation curve of (c) CCDs dispersion in ethanol and (d) the mixture of CCDs dispersion in ethanol and FeCl3 aqueous solution (3000 μM). (e) The mechanism diagram of fluorescence quenching of CCDs dispersion by FeCl3 solution.

(a) SEM image and (b) FT-IR spectrum of 4 % CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane. (c) The tensile stress–strain curves of different PA56 nanofiber membrane. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of 4 % CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane under different excitation lights. (e) Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 470 nm) and (d) CIE coordinates of five kinds of CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane.

(a) Digital images, (b) fluorescence emission spectra and (c) fluorescence intensity ratios I0/I of FNM4 (control) and soaked membrane with different metal salt aqueous solution (1000 μM, λex = 470 nm). (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of soaked FNM4 with different FeCl3 aqueous solutions (0–3000 μM, λex = 470 nm). (e) Relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I of FNM4 and the concentration of FeCl3 solution.

(a) Digital images (iPhone 11) of soaked FNM4 in FeCl3 aqueous solutions with different concentrations under visible light and 365 nm UV light. (b) Relationship between B value of soaked FNM4′s image (under visible light) and FeCl3 concentration. Relationship between(c) R value, (d) G value and (e) B value of soaked FNM4′s images (under UV light, 365 nm) and FeCl3 concentration.

(a) Digital images (mate 30 and Xiaomi 13) of soaked FNM4 in FeCl3 aqueous solutions with different concentrations under visible light and UV light (365 nm). Relationship between Fe3+ concentration and RGB values of membrane images taken by Huawei smartphone (b, c, d) and Xiaomi smartphone (e, f, g) when irradiated by UV light.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of CCDs powder and CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane.
Comparison with other Fe3+ detection methods.

head.ce:title: Application of polyamide 56 nanofiber membrane loaded with coffee grounds carbon dots in Fe3+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory for Modern Silk, College of Textile and Clothing Engineering, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory for Modern Silk
College of Textile and Clothing Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Laboratory for Modern Silk, College of Textile and Clothing Engineering, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One novel nanofiber membrane was fabricated which could detect Fe3+ with the help of either fluorescence spectrometer or smartphone. Coffee grounds carbon dots (CCDs) were prepared by the solvent-thermal method, followed by the fabrication of CCDs/polyamide 56 (PA56) composite nanofiber membrane through electrospinning process. The 4 % CCDs/PA56 composite nanofiber membrane (FNM4) maintained good fluorescence performance (λem = 554 nm, λex = 470 nm) even after 5-runs quenching-recovery cycles. Its fluorescence could be quenched by Fe3+ with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.74 μM when the fluorescence spectrometer was used. In addition, FNM4 could also detect Fe3+ by reading its RGB values using one smartphone and the lowest LOD was 4.31 μM when one Apple smartphone was used. The second method is portable to operate and eliminates the constraints of bulky laboratory equipment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron not only participates in the exchange and transportation of oxygen in the blood, but also serves as vital element for human enzymes [1]. But accumulation of excessive iron ions in the body can lead to organ damage [2,3]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop one effective method for detecting concentration of iron ions. According to the guidelines for drinking water quality reported by World Health Organization (WHO), the upper limit for iron ions in water is 5.4 μM [4]. Traditional methods for iron ions detection included fluorescence sensing [5], electrochemical analysis [6], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [7]. Among photoluminescent nanomaterials used for fluorescence sensing, biomass carbon dots (BCDs) possess advantages such as environmentally friendly nature, rich carbon sources, strong tunability, and good biocompatibility [8–11]. For example, fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) was synthesized using Phyllanthus acidus by hydrothermal method [5], FNCDs had durable fluorescence, was soluble in water very well and could be used for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions in a wide concentration range of 2–25 μM with the detection limit of 0.9 μM. The majority of current fluorometric methods for detecting Fe3+ using carbon dots (CDs) relied on the single CDs aqueous solution as the standard indicator. However, these CDs had the tendency to agglomerate over time during testing, leading to potential inaccuracies in measurements [12].

Nanofiber membrane possess suitable mechanical properties, high specific surface area, and high porosity [13]. By loading biomass carbon dots onto nanofiber membrane, the instability of fluorescence signals caused by carbon dot aggregation could be avoided and environmental interference could be reduced [14]. Currently, polyamide nanofibers could be used in sensors [15,16], nanogenerator [17,18] and air filtration [19,20]. In the field of heavy metal ion detection, Migliorini et al. [15] utilized gold nanoclusters to modify PA6/chitosan nanofibers and deposited them onto FTO conductive glass substrates to form sensors for highly sensitive detection of Cd(II), with the LOD of 0.88 μg/L (detection range: 25–75 μg/L). Polyamide 56 (PA56) nanofiber, one environmentally friendly biomaterial nanofiber, possess excellent mechanical properties, good hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility [21,22]. Therefore, it offered unique advantages when PA56 nanofiber was chosen as the substrate for the preparation of CDs-based fluorescence probes for detecting iron ions.

Fluorescence sensing, electrochemical analysis and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry for detecting iron ions required bulky detection equipment normally, which were expensive to maintain and involved tedious sample processing [23,24]. Currently, cost-effective & portable method is an increasingly urgent demand for iron ions detection.

Here, Fe3+ sensitive coffee grounds carbon points (CCDs) were synthesized by the solvothermal process. By further combining CCDs with PA56 nanofiber membrane via electrospinning, the nanofiber membrane could detect Fe3+ based on both the fluorescence intensity recorded by fluorescence spectrometer and the RGB channel value read by smartphone.

The coffee grounds were sourced from the convenience store chain FamilyMart. Polyamide 56 were self-made (Molecular weight = 17200). Formic acid (HCOOH) and ethanol were purchased from Jiangsu Qiangsheng Functional Chemical Co., Ltd. (China), and ascorbic acid (AA) was purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. CuCl2, FeCl3, AgNO3, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, FeCl2·4H2O, LiCl, ZnCl2, AlCl3·6H2O, and (CH3COO)2Mn were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. MgCl2 was purchased from Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co., Ltd (China). All these materials were used as received without further purification.

CCDs were prepared by one solvothermal method as outlined in Scheme 1 . Firstly, coffee grounds were dried overnight in one air drying oven (60℃) to remove some residual moisture. Secondly, the mixture of dried coffee grounds (1 g) in 50 mL deionized water & ethanol (1:1 by volume) was stirred at room temperature for 1 h, and then transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave before solvothermal reaction (200℃, 12 h). The solvothermal reaction product was then collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through the 0.22 μm membrane to remove large impurities. The filtrate was then dialyzed for 72 h using the 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove small molecular impurities [25,26]. Finally, the dialyzed product was freeze dried for 72 h to obtain purified coffee grounds carbon dots, which was brown powder and named as CCDs.

The process for the fabrication of CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane is also illustrated in Scheme 1. CCDs (the mass ratio to PA56 was 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, 4 %, and 5 %) were added to PA56 solution (WPA56: WHCOOH = 22:78) and the mixture was stirred for 12 h. Subsequently, the resulted spinning dope was transferred into 10 mL glass syringe which was then secured to the syringe pump. The spinning voltage was set as 15 kV and spinning rate as 0.08 mL/h. The distance from the needle tip to the collector was set as 15 cm. The ambient temperature and humidity were maintained at 25℃ and 50 %, respectively. The spinning process lasted for 12 h. Finally, the spun nanofiber membrane was transferred into air drying oven (60℃, 12 h) and the resulted sample was named as CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane.

The field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained by Hitachi SU8230 (Japan). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained by Tecnai G2 F20 (USA). The Raman spectrometer (LabRAM Soleil, Horiba, France) equipped with one 100 mW laser emitting at 532 nm was used to obtain the Raman spectra. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by D8 Advance (Bruker, Germany) with Cu–K alpha radiation under the radiation voltage of 40 kV. The Fourier-transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by Vertex80 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed using ESCALAB 250 Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA) with the Al target. The mechanical property of the membrane was tested by the electronic universal testing machine (INSTRON-5965, USA). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, England). Fluorescence quantum yields were measured using absolute methods.

2 mL metal salt (CuCl2, FeCl3, AgNO3, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, FeCl2, LiCl, ZnCl2, AlCl3, (CH3COO)2Mn) aqueous solution (1000 μM) was added into 2 mL CCDs ethanol dispersion (0.1 mg/mL) separately for CCDs selective study. To determine the LOD of Fe3+, several FeCl3 aqueous solution (0–3000 μM, 2 mL) were added into CCDs ethanol dispersion (0.1 mg/mL, 2 mL). All the mixture was then sonicated for 5 min to ensure homogeneity, and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the mixture was measured. For CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane, they should be soaked in different metal ion aqueous solutions (1000 μM) and FeCl3 aqueous solutions (0–3000 μM) for 1 h and then removed out to dry for testing.

To achieve on-site detection, one portable detection platform was constructed in this experiment (Fig. S1). As the UV lamp was turned on, different soaked CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane samples were photographed using smartphones (iPhone 11, Huawei Mate30 and Xiaomi 13) and the RGB values of each membrane were then obtained using color recognition software on the smartphone. By recording the corresponding relationship between the set FeCl3 aqueous solution’s concentration and the RGB value of the soaked nanofiber membrane, quantitative detection of Fe3+ could be achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitor Fe3+ in different water sources
ce:section.ce:para: Using the fluorescence spectrophotometer and the smartphone detection platform, Fe3+ detection was also carried out in tap water and lake water using the standard addition method. The specific procedure is as follows: Lake water was obtained from Dushu Lake (The detailed procedures are described in SI). Tap water was obtained directly from the laboratory and did not require further treatment. FeCl3 aqueous solutions (100 μM, 200 μM) were prepared in deionized water, tap water and lake water, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra of soaked CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane were recorded by the fluorescence spectrometer, while the RGB values of sample’s image were obtained using the color recognition software on the smartphone.

Size and morphology of CCDs particles were evaluated using TEM, which were evenly distributed throughout the field of view without aggregation. The CCDs powder were brown (Fig. S2, inset), and spherical particles (around 20 nm, Fig. S2) could be found which may be attributed to the assembly of several smaller nanosheets. They could be dispersed well in ethanol or ethanol–water mixtures (1:1 by volume) (Fig. S3). The XRD pattern (Fig. 1 a) showed one characteristic graphitic carbon diffraction peak (2θ = 21°) associated with the (1 0 0) plane of graphite [27], similar to the lattice spacing of graphite (sp2) carbon [28], which was attributed to the uneven arrangement of carbon atoms and weakly crystalline carbon. The Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 1b) further revealed the structure and degree of graphitization of the CCDs. The Raman spectrum of the CCDs consisted of D band (1360 cm−1) and G band (1570 cm−1).The D band was related to edge distortions and structural defects in the graphene structure, and the G band indicated the presence of graphite phase [29]. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) was calculated to be 0.76 and indicated the presence of defects inside or at the edges of the CCDs [30].

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1c) revealed broad absorption band at 3300–3400 cm−1, which was attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H [25,31], indicating the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CCDs. The amino groups on CCDs had the potential to form intermolecular hydrogen bonds with the amide bonds of PA56, enhancing the compatibility between CCDs and PA56, which was very beneficial for the subsequent preparation of CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane. Furthermore, peaks observed at 1650, 1393, and 1058 cm−1 may be attributed to the vibrational absorption of CO, C(O)O, and C-O, respectively [27,32]. Moreover, the XPS full spectrum (Fig. S4) of the CCDs powder revealed three characteristic peaks at 284.8, 399.7, and 532.2 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum of CCDs (Fig. 1d) displayed peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and C(O)O functional groups, respectively [33]. The O1s spectrum of CCDs (Fig. 1e) displayed peaks at 533.5 and 532 eV (corresponding to O–H/C–O and C(O)O), while the N1s XPS spectrum of CCDs (Fig. 1f) revealed the presence of nitrogen atoms, primarily in the form of pyrrole N (399.6 eV) and graphite N (400.1 eV) [34].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the dispersion of CCDs in ethanol (Fig. 2 a) showed that there was the shoulder peak around 278 nm, which may be originated from the π → π* transition of CC [32,35]. The fluorescence quantum yield is 4.52 %. When the excitation wavelength was 400 nm, the emission peak of the dispersion located at 497 nm (Fig. 2a). The fluorescence emission spectra of CCDs dispersion exhibited dependence on the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2b and S5a). These phenomena may be related to the presence of different functional groups on the surface of CCDs. Different functional groups may act as multiple fluorescence emission centers, and the dominating fluorescence emission centers may be different under different excitation lights, resulting in changes in emission peak intensity and peak position.

Fig. 2c presented the fluorescence emission spectra of the CCDs dispersion at different concentrations (λex = 400 nm). The fluorescence intensity increased initially with the increase of CCDs’ concentration, but CCDs’ fluorescence intensity decreased when its concentration was over 0.10 mg/mL due to fluorescence self-quenching [36,37]. The continuous redshift of the fluorescence emission peak (Fig. S5b) existed and which might be attributed to the aggregation of CCDs. The CIE coordinates of these fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 2d) also demonstrated the change in fluorescence zone from blue to green.

In unknown aqueous solutions, multiple metal ions often coexist, necessitating precise identification by sensors. Digital images of the mixture from various metal salt aqueous solutions and CCDs ethanol dispersion were taken, as shown in Fig. 3 a. Under visible light, the addition of FeCl3 aqueous solution to CCDs ethanol dispersion resulted in discernible deepening of the mixture's color. The cyan-green fluorescence of the mixture decreased significantly under UV light (365 nm). However, other metal salt aqueous solutions had less effect on the color and fluorescence of CCDs dispersion. The results (Fig. 3b and c) revealed that Fe3+ significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of CCDs dispersion while other ions had less effect, indicating the higher selectivity of CCDs towards Fe3+. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectra of CCDs dispersion in the presence of interfering ions and Fe3+ were tested. As illustrated in Fig. S6, there was no significant alteration in the fluorescence intensity ratio of the mixture, which indicated that CCDs could detect Fe3+ in multi-ion solutions.

Digital images of the mixture from FeCl3 solution (0–3000 μM) and CCDs dispersion were taken (Fig. 3d). As the concentration of Fe3+ increased, the mixture’s color under visible light gradually deepened from pale yellow to deep yellow and the cyan-green fluorescence under UV light diminished gradually. As shown in Fig. 3e, the fluorescence intensity decreased with FeCl3 concentration and eventually approached quenching. The linear relationship (I0/I = 0.00472[Fe3+] + 1.00136, R2 = 0.996) could be found between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I0/I) of CCDs and the concentration of FeCl3 solution (0–600 μM). According to the formula (LOD = 3δ/S) [38], the LOD was calculated to be 0.93 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism study of CCD dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the mechanism of CCDs fluorescence quenching, the UV absorption spectroscopy test was conducted on the mixture from CCDs ethanol dispersion and different FeCl3 aqueous solution. The obvious absorption peak of FeCl3 solution (Vethanol: Vwater = 1:1) was at 297 nm (Fig. 4 a), while one shoulder peak located at 278 nm (Fig. 4b) could be found in the spectrum of the CCDs dispersion (Vethanol: Vwater = 1:1). As the concentration of FeCl3 aqueous solution increased to 800 μM, one can find new emerging absorption peak (248–265 nm) and their intensity increased gradually with the FeCl3 concentration until that concentration reached 2000 μM (Fig. 4b). The phenomena may be attributed to the increasing of the complex of formed between Fe3+ and surface groups of CCDs, which led to the quenching of CCDs fluorescence, akin to the phenomenon of non-fluorescent complex induced static quenching as reported in literatures [39–41]. As the concentration of FeCl3 solution increased over 2000 μM, the absorption peak of FeCl3 gradually became more prominent in the mixture, indicating that the above-mentioned complex had reached equilibrium while the effects of competitive absorption from Fe3+ continued.

Fig. 4c and d showed the fluorescence decay curves of the CCD ethanol dispersion and the mixture from CCD ethanol dispersion and FeCl3 aqueous solution (3000 μM). The fluorescence lifetime of two samples was 1.78 ns and 1.77 ns, respectively, which meant that the additional FeCl3 did not have much effect on the fluorescence lifetime of the CCD dispersion. The test result was consistent with the phenomenon of static quenching [11], which further confirmed that the quenching of CCD fluorescence by Fe3+ in this manuscript belonged to static quenching.

Two reasons for the fluorescence quenching of CCDs by Fe3+ could be suggested as shown in Fig. 4e. On the one hand, the non-fluorescent complex formed between Fe3+ and the amino & carboxyl groups on the surface of CCDs. On the other hand, Fe3+ could absorb exciting light and then CCDs has less chance of being excited, which was named as competitive absorption here. Both factors played the role when the concentrations of FeCl3 was lower, then fluorescence intensity decreased significantly with the increase of FeCl3′s concentration. When the concentrations of FeCl3 was high enough, non-fluorescent complexes reached saturation but competitive absorption continued, then fluorescence intensity decreased slowly.

The SEM images showed that the addition of CCDs did not change significantly the morphology of the nanofiber membrane (Fig. 5a and S7), but the diameter of the nanofibers showed one increasing trend with the CCDs content (image inserted in Fig. 5 a). The FT-IR spectrum of the 4 % CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane (Fig. 6 b) revealed one peak at 3310 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of N–H bonds, while two peaks at 1648 cm−1 and 1537 cm−1 were attributed to the amide I band (CO) and the amide II band (N–H), respectively [42]. Additionally, the peak at 1270 cm−1 signified the stretching vibration of CH2-NH [42]. The CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane exhibited high hydrophilicity due to the presence of amide bonds, which was conducive to the detection of iron ions in aqueous environments. The introduction of CCDs appeared to affect slightly the mechanical properties of the membrane, as illustrated in Fig. 6c. The fluorescence emission spectra of 4 % CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane (Fig. 5d) exhibited excitation dependence. At the excitation wavelength of 470 nm, the emission peak located at 554 nm (Fig. 5d) and fluorescence intensity reached the highest point. The concentration of CCDs also had influence on the fluorescence emission spectrum of the membrane (Fig. 5e). With the increase of CCDs concentration, the emission peak redshifted continuously and the membrane’s fluorescence intensity increased firstly and then decreased. This may be due to the aggregation of CCDs at higher concentrations within the PA56 matrix, resulting in π-orbital overlap of CCDs [43,44]. CIE coordinates also showed the nanofiber membrane’s fluorescence redshift with CCDs concentration as shown in Fig. 5f. In addition, FNM4 maintained good fluorescence intensity at different pH values, after UV irradiation for 180 min or after storage for 30 days at room temperature, which showed good fluorescence stability (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane for Fe3+ detection
Fe3+ detection based on fluorescence spectrometer
ce:section.ce:para: The variation in fluorescence intensity of the CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane with soaking time in FeCl3 aqueous solution (3000 μM) was first studied. The fluorescence intensity decreased with soaking time and reached steady at 60 min (Fig. S9a). To select the most sensitive nanofiber membrane to Fe3+, three types of nanofiber membrane with good fluorescence intensity (3 % CCDs/PA56, 4 % CCDs/PA56, 5 % CCDs/PA56) were chose to compare their detection sensitivity. When Fe3+ concentration was 500 μM, the fluorescence intensity of 3 % CCDs/PA56, 4 % CCDs/PA56 and 5 % CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane decreased by 56.3 %, 64.8 % and 62.1 %, respectively, and the fluorescence intensity of 4 % CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane decreased the most (Fig. S9b). So, the nanofiber membrane with 4 % CCDs concentration was selected for subsequent detection and labeled as FNM4.

The selectivity of FNM4 for different metal ions was investigated. Digital images of FNM4 after soaking in different metal salt aqueous solutions were taken under visible light and 365 nm UV light (Fig. 6a). The color of FNM4 changed to yellower after adsorption of Fe3+ under visible light, while the fluorescence brightness significantly decreased under UV light. Both fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 6b) and fluorescence intensity ratios (Fig. 6c) showed the fluorescence intensity of FNM4 decreased significantly after adsorbing Fe3+, while the influence of other metal ion on the fluorescence intensity of the membrane was not significant. When FNM4 was soaked in FeCl3 aqueous solution, the nanofibers adsorbed Fe3+ from the surrounding solution and membrane’s color changed into yellow gradually. In addition, its fluorescence intensity decreased due to the complexation and competitive absorption above-mentioned.

The quenching of the fluorescence of FNM4 by different FeCl3 aqueous solutions (0–3000 μM) was then investigated. Fig. 6d illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of the nanofiber membrane decreased as the concentration of FeCl3 solution increased. There was one linear relationship (R2 = 0.994) between the ratio of fluorescence intensities (I0/I) and Fe3+ concentration (0–500 μM), and the fitting curve was represented by I0/I = 0.00349[Fe3+] + 0.932 (Fig. 6e). According to the formula (LOD = 3δ/S), the LOD was calculated to be 0.74 μM, which was lower than the maximum concentration of iron ions (5.4 μM) reported in the drinking water quality guidelines of the WHO [4]. In this sense, FNM4 fabricated in this experiment can be used to detect whether the concentration of Fe3+ in water exceeded the WHO standard.

It has been reported that ascorbic acid (AA) can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ [26]. Even Fe3+ quenched the fluorescence of FNM4, the membrane’s fluorescence could restore when it was washed with AA solution (1000 μM). Following five uses, the fluorescence intensity of the membrane remained above 80 % (Fig. S10), indicating that FNM4 was one suitable fluorescent sensing material with the potential for reuse. Fig. S11 showed the fluorescence attenuation curve of FNM4 before and after soaking in FeCl3 solutions. The fluorescence lifetime of two samples was 3.45 ns and 3.62 ns, respectively. The experimental results showed little change in fluorescence lifetime, which is consistent with our previous observation of static bursting of CCDs.

From the perspective of on-site applications, there is need to develop one portable Fe3+ detection analysis device. Here, we constructed one portable platform based on smartphones. Fig. S1 showed all accessories. In addition, the detection operation of the smartphone platform was shown (Video S1). Fig. 7 a depicted images of FNM4 soaked in different concentrations of FeCl3 aqueous solutions. It was evident that the yellow color of soaked FNM4 deepened gradually under visible light as the concentration of the FeCl3 solution increased, while the fluorescence brightness under UV light faded gradually to almost disappear. Under the same shooting conditions (iso, time of exposure, aperture value), the lighter the color of the membrane image and the smaller the RGB value [23]. This laid the foundation for the detection of Fe3+ on one smartphone. The color recognition software on the smartphone could identify the RGB value of FNM4′s image. The relationship between the RGB value of FNM4′s image and the Fe3+ concentration was established as shown in Fig. 7b–e.

Under visible light, the color of nanofiber membrane deepened gradually with Fe3+ concentration, and the B values of the nanofiber membrane images decreased and exhibited linear relationship (R2 = 0.997) within Fe3+ concentration (0–3000 μM) as illustrated in Fig. 7b. Under UV light (365 nm), the fluorescence brightness of nanofiber membrane gradually became weaker with Fe3+ concentration and the RGB value decreased synchronously. The calculation and fitting process indicated that the response relationship between the RGB value of the nanofiber membrane and the concentration of Fe3+ was different in the two concentration ranges (0–400 μM and 400–3000 μM), as shown in Fig. 7c–e. Based on the formula (LOD = 3δ/S), the optimal LOD was calculated to be 4.31 μM (according to R value). In this way, CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane can be used for Fe3+ on-site detection with the help of smartphones.

The smartphones of different brands often take different colors of the same object due to variations in imaging hardware and algorithms. In addition to Apple smartphone, Huawei and Xiaomi smartphone was also used to take images of nanofiber membrane under UV light. During the taking process, the nanofiber membrane was placed in the dark box to eliminate the influence of ambient light, where the ISO sensitivity was set to 6400, shutter speed to 1/40, color temperature to 5200°K, and aperture to constant. Fig. 8 a showed the nanofiber membrane’s images taken by Huawei smartphone (mate 30) and Xiaomi smartphone (Xiaomi 13) under UV light. The colors of images taken by two smartphones were different. As the concentration of Fe3+ increased, the fluorescence brightness of the nanofiber membrane gradually faded, and its RGB values decreased accordingly. The linear relationship between Fe3+ concentration and the RGB value of membrane’s images taken by Huawei smartphone was shown in Fig. 8b–d. The optimal LOD of the Huawei smartphone was 11.83 μM (according to R value). The linear relationship between Fe3+ concentration and the RGB value of membranes images taken by Xiaomi smartphone was shown in Fig. 8e–g. The optimal LOD of the Xiaomi smartphone was 9.90 μM (according to R value). In other words, different brands of smartphones could have the potential to detect Fe3+, although the imaging hardware and algorithms were different.

FNM4 was employed to detect Fe3+ in deionized water, tap water and lake water using the standard addition method. The FeCl3 aqueous solutions (100 μM and 200 μM) were prepared in deionized water, tap water and lake water, respectively. The detection data were recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer and smartphone respectively, as shown in Table 1 . For the detection of Fe3+ by fluorescence spectrophotometry, the detection recoveries of 100 μM and 200 μM FeCl3 solutions were 99.1 % and 98.3 % (deionized water), 105.4 % and 96 % (tap water), 94 % and 95.6 % (lake water), respectively. For the detection of Fe3+ by smartphone, the detection recoveries of 100 μM and 200 μM FeCl3 solutions were 99.2 % and 97.1 % (deionized water), 107.5 % and 104.7 % (tap water), 104.4 % and 98.5 % (lake water), respectively. The results of both methods matched closely to the spiked values with RSDs less than 5.12 %, demonstrating the reliability of FNM4 for Fe3+ detection.

The LOD, recovery and RSD are important parameters for evaluating the performance of sensing materials. The comparison was made between FNM4 prepared in this experiment and other sensing materials in literatures, as shown in Table 2 . One can see that FNM4 was comparable to those published materials. Moreover, FNM4 prepared with biomass materials in this experiment also had other characteristics such as low cost, environmental friendliness, and simple operation.

The effective CCDs/PA56 nanofiber membrane was prepared using biomass materials for the quantitative detection of Fe3+. The CCDs were prepared via solvothermal method and exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ due to static quenching of the complex and competitive absorption of Fe3+. 4 % CCDs/PA56 composite nanofiber membrane (FNM4) could detect Fe3+ using the fluorescence spectrometer with the LOD of 0.74 μM. It was noteworthy that FNM4 could also detect Fe3+ with one smartphone, showing the practicality of on-site detection with the LOD of 4.31 μ M when iPhone 11 was used. Smartphone-based detection provided one portable and efficient method for Fe3+ detection that might be extended to other analytical science.

Baichuan Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Visualization, Data curation. Zhaoxuan Liu: Validation, Data curation. Dongliang Qian: Validation, Data curation. Jun Sun: Visualization, Formal analysis. Jian-jun Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Chuanxiang Qin: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision. Lixing Dai: Resources, Supervision. Guoqiang Chen: Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Key Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019001-3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125440.

ce:section.ce:para:
